ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to register to vote in the AZ July 21 primary.
ELECTION_EVENTS.notes: Must be received or postmarked by June 22, 2026 (29 days before election).

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Mail ballots delivered to Active Early Voters List (AEVL). In-person early voting opens at county recorders.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: County Recorders (Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, Pinal)

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to request a mail ballot for the primary.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders

ELECTION_EVENTS.milestone: Last day of in-person early voting
ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to vote in-person early at county recorder offices before Election Day.
Arizona statewide primary. Polls open 6 AM – 7 PM local time.
Mail ballot must be physically RECEIVED by county recorder by 7:00 PM. Postmarks do not count.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders
ELECTION_EVENTS.notes: Arizona does NOT accept postmarks — ballot must be in hand at recorder by 7:00 PM on election day.

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Cure missing or mismatched signatures on returned mail ballots (5 business days after election).
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to register to vote before the November 3 general election.
Mail ballots sent to AEVL. In-person early voting opens at county recorders.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: County Recorders (Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, Pinal)

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to request a mail ballot for the general election.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders

ELECTION_EVENTS.milestone: Last day of in-person early voting
ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to vote in-person early at county recorder offices before the general election.
U.S. midterm general election. Polls open 6 AM – 7 PM local time.
Mail ballot must be physically RECEIVED by county recorder by 7:00 PM. Postmarks do not count.
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Cure missing or mismatched signatures on returned mail ballots (5 business days after general election).
ELECTION_EVENTS.authority: Arizona Secretary of State / County Recorders

RULES.currentRule: Online, mail, or in-person registration. Must be a U.S. citizen, AZ resident, 18+ by election day. Registration deadline is 29 days before the election.
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines who can cast a ballot in AZ-06. Voters must register with their county recorder — Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, or Pinal.
RULES.detail: Primary registration deadline: June 22, 2026. General registration deadline: October 5, 2026. Voters can register online if they have an AZ driver license or state ID.

RULES.currentRule: Documentary proof of citizenship required to vote a full ballot in state and local races. SCOTUS partial stay on HB 2492 granted May 10, 2026 — status for the Oct 5 general registration deadline is unresolved.
RULES.whyItMatters: Federal-only voters who lack documentary proof receive a restricted federal-only ballot. Monitor closely through summer 2026 as SCOTUS considers the case.
RULES.detail: RNC v. Mi Familia Vota. 9th Circuit had struck down HB 2492; SCOTUS granted partial stay. Cert petition responses due May 26. Affects all AZ-06 counties equally.

RULES.currentRule: Photo ID or two non-photo IDs with name and address. Out-of-precinct voters cast a provisional ballot. AZ-06 counties use precinct-based voting, not vote centers.
RULES.whyItMatters: Affects in-person eligibility. Provisional ballots cast in the wrong precinct may not be counted for all races.

RULES.currentRule: Any voter registered in Arizona may request an early ballot from their county recorder or on the Secretary of State’s website.
RULES.whyItMatters: Mail voting is widely used across all AZ-06 counties. Each county recorder processes requests independently.

RULES.currentRule: Voters on the AEVL automatically receive a mail ballot for every election. Join or update status through your county recorder or the statewide voter portal.
RULES.whyItMatters: AEVL is the primary mechanism for recurring mail ballots in AZ-06. Voters who drop off the list must re-request each election.
RULES.detail: ARS § 16-544. Pima County: pima.vote. Statewide: my.arizona.vote. Contact Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, or Pinal recorders for county-specific enrollment.

RULES.currentRule: Early voting begins 27 days before the election, and you can vote in person at an early voting location or by mail. To vote early in person, go to any early voting location in your county during its early voting period as listed by your county recorder; locations and whether they operate as vote centers or precinct-based sites are determined by the county and are not specified by the Secretary of State.
RULES.whyItMatters: Provides an alternative to election-day voting. Sites and hours vary by county — verify with Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, or Pinal recorder.

RULES.currentRule: AZ-06 counties use precinct-based voting. Voters must vote at their assigned precinct polling place. Voting at the wrong precinct will result in a provisional ballot.
RULES.whyItMatters: Unlike Maricopa (vote centers), AZ-06 voters cannot vote at any open polling location — precinct assignment matters.
RULES.detail: Pinal County precinct court ruling (Votebeat, May 2026) may affect provisional ballot handling for out-of-precinct voters. Monitor implementation across all AZ-06 counties.

RULES.currentRule: AZ-06 boundary set by the 2021 Independent Redistricting Commission map for the 2022–2030 cycle. Covers Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, and portions of Pinal County — no Maricopa County component.
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines who is a constituent of AZ-06. Confirm your county and precinct with your county recorder.

RULES.currentRule: July 21, 2026 is the controlling primary election date for AZ-06 voter-facing deadlines. Any Aug. 4, 2026 references from older sources are superseded.
RULES.whyItMatters: Prevents stale Aug. 4 references from driving registration, early voting, mail-ballot request, or ballot-receipt deadlines for AZ-06 voters.
RULES.detail: Arizona HB2022 moved the 2026 primary from Aug. 4 to July 21. General election remains November 3, 2026.

RULES.currentRule: Mail ballot requests must be received by the county recorder at least 11 days before election day. For the 2026 primary, the deadline is July 10.
RULES.whyItMatters: Late requests are rejected; voter must use in-person early voting or election-day precinct voting.

RULES.currentRule: Mail ballots must be received (not postmarked) by 7:00 PM on election day by the county recorder. Primary receipt deadline: July 21 at 7 PM. General: November 3 at 7 PM.
RULES.whyItMatters: Most common cause of ballot rejection in AZ. Postmark does not count — physical receipt by 7 PM is required.
RULES.detail: ARS § 16-548. Ballots may be dropped at any county recorder office, early voting site, or official drop box by 7 PM election day.

RULES.currentRule: Common rejection causes: late receipt, missing signature, signature mismatch, voter not registered at address on file.
RULES.whyItMatters: Affects whether a returned ballot is counted. AZ-06 voters should verify their ballot was accepted after the election.

RULES.currentRule: For 2026 federal-office elections, voters may cure signature issues during a five-calendar-day post-election window. Primary cure deadline: July 26. General: November 8.
RULES.whyItMatters: Curing window directly determines whether a signature-defect ballot is counted. Monitor Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, and Pinal county notification processes.
RULES.detail: ARS § 16-550. HB2022 confirms the 2026 federal-office cure window uses calendar days. Retain as watch until each AZ-06 county recorder publishes election-specific cure notification guidance.

RULES.currentRule: County canvass within 20 days of election day; statewide canvass certifies official results.
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines when results become official across all AZ-06 counties.

RULES.currentRule: Automatic recount triggered when the margin is at or below the statutory threshold.
RULES.whyItMatters: Determines whether a recount runs and on what timeline for AZ-06 races.

UPDATES.title: AZ primary date moved to July 21 — new law signed Feb 6, 2026
UPDATES.newValue: July 21, 2026 (second-to-last Tuesday in July, per new statute signed by Gov. Katie Hobbs Feb 6, 2026)

UPDATES.title: SCOTUS partial stay granted on AZ proof-of-citizenship requirement (May 10, 2026)
UPDATES.oldValue: 9th Circuit injunction blocking HB 2492 in effect
UPDATES.newValue: SCOTUS granted partial stay May 10, 2026; cert petition responses due May 26. Requirement status uncertain for 2026 general election.

UPDATES.title: AZ-06 config initialized — all primary and general deadlines wired
UPDATES.newValue: All 2026 election events, sources (14), rules, and resources configured for initial release.

UPDATES.title: 2026 deadlines re-verified against AZ Secretary of State; no changes detected
UPDATES.newValue: As of the June 17, 2026 refresh, AZ SOS guidance still lists July 21, 2026 as the primary election (per HB 2022, signed Feb. 6, 2026), June 22, 2026 as the primary voter registration deadline, June 24 – July 17, 2026 as the early in-person voting window, July 10, 2026 as the mail-ballot application deadline, and November 3, 2026 as Election Day. Signature curing window remains five calendar days after election per HB 2022. No changes detected for AZ-06 counties (Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, Pinal).

UPDATES.title: HCR 2001 voter-ID ballot measure formally placed on November 2026 ballot
UPDATES.oldValue: Voter-ID expansion proposals were tracked as pending legislative action.
UPDATES.newValue: On June 15, 2026, the Arizona legislature formally placed HCR 2001 on the November 3, 2026 ballot, bypassing Gov. Hobbs's veto authority for legislatively-referred constitutional amendments. If passed by voters, the measure would require government-issued ID for all voters — including mail voters — and make additional election-rule changes. Does NOT change current July 21, 2026 primary procedures or November 3, 2026 general procedures. Watch-only item for AZ-06 voters.

CONFLICTS.title: Proof-of-citizenship requirement — conflicting court orders
CONFLICTS.description: Arizona HB 2492 (proof-of-citizenship to register) has conflicting federal and state court rulings. 9th Circuit struck it down; SCOTUS granted partial stay May 10, 2026. The law's status for the November 2026 general election registration deadline (Oct 5) is unresolved.

NEWS.headline: Democrat JoAnna Mendoza leads incumbent Ciscomani in AZ-06 polls
NEWS.relevanceSummary: A conservative-leaning poll (Ragnar Research, April 2026) shows Mendoza (D) leading Ciscomani (R) 47%-44%. Mendoza raised $2.44M in Q1 2026 vs. Ciscomani's $1.14M. Prediction markets favor Mendoza at 76%. July 21 primary precedes the November 3 general.

NEWS.headline: Trump DOJ backs Arizona proof-of-citizenship voting law at U.S. Supreme Court
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Trump DOJ asked SCOTUS to allow Arizona to block voters without proof of citizenship and enable county roll purges. Could affect AZ-06 general election voter registration, particularly in Cochise and Pima counties along the border.

NEWS.headline: Ciscomani vs. Mendoza: AZ-06 enters primary stretch as Cook rates the race a toss-up
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Race trackers continue to rate AZ-06 a toss-up heading into the July 21 primary. Incumbent Juan Ciscomani (R) faces DCCC-backed JoAnna Mendoza (D) on Nov. 3. Cook Political Report has the district at EVEN partisan lean; Mendoza led Ciscomani 47-44 in an April Ragnar Research poll. Both campaigns are spending heavily on Pima County media.

NEWS.headline: Arizona ballot measure to change voting rules will be on the November 2026 ballot
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Arizona lawmakers placed a November 2026 ballot measure (HCR 2001) before voters that would require ID for mail voters and make other election-rule changes if approved. For AZ-06 voters in Pima, Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, and Pinal counties, treat this as watch-list news only; it does not change current July 21 primary or November 3 general procedures.

NEWS.headline: Federal Judge Dismisses DOJ Lawsuit Seeking Arizona Voter Rolls
NEWS.relevanceSummary: U.S. District Judge Susan Brnovich dismissed the DOJ's lawsuit demanding Arizona's unredacted statewide voter registration list. The 6th federal court to reject Trump voter roll demands; protects AZ-06 voter privacy.

NEWS.headline: Supreme Court Partially Reinstates Arizona's Proof-of-Citizenship Voting Law
NEWS.relevanceSummary: SCOTUS granted a partial stay of injunctions blocking Arizona's HB 2492 proof-of-citizenship requirement. Cert petition responses due May 26. A ruling could affect 2026 general election voter registration in AZ-06.

NEWS.headline: Arizona Primary Election Challenges Remove 12 Candidates From Ballot
NEWS.relevanceSummary: 22 candidates challenged across AZ statewide, congressional, and legislative races. 12 removed. Ballots finalized and sent to print May 11. No AZ-06-specific challenges noted.

NEWS.headline: Campaign Legal Center Challenges Trump's Executive Order on Vote-by-Mail
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Federal lawsuit filed to block Trump executive order (March 31) restricting vote-by-mail and creating a national voter registry. AZ Students' Association is a plaintiff — directly relevant to AZ-06 voters who rely on AEVL/mail voting.

NEWS.headline: Maricopa County Power Struggle Over Election Control Threatens 2026 Preparedness
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Legal dispute between Maricopa Recorder and Board of Supervisors over election control. Note: AZ-06 has NO Maricopa County, but statewide AZ SOS infrastructure and shared ballot systems may be indirectly affected.

NEWS.headline: Brennan Center: Arizona Among States With Most Restrictive Voting Laws Since 2021
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Arizona among states enacting 5+ restrictive voting laws since 2021. FBI and DOJ targeted AZ election records. Proposed federal immigration officers at polling places (not enacted). Relevant to AZ-06 border-area counties Cochise and Greenlee.

NEWS.headline: Supreme Court will weigh Trump-backed Republican push to revive Arizona voting laws
NEWS.relevanceSummary: The article explains that the U.S. Supreme Court will hear a case on Arizona’s proof-of-citizenship requirement for voter registration, which already applies to state and local elections but not federal races, meaning AZ-06 voters may continue to face different documentation rules depending on which contests they vote in and that those rules could change after the Court’s decision.[1]

NEWS.headline: Upcoming deadlines for Chandler Primary Election
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Provides specific early voting dates, ballot drop-off hours, and Election Day vote center locations in Chandler, a city within AZ-06, including information on using Chandler City Hall and other vote centers for in-person voting and ballot drop-off and how to request a replacement ballot from Maricopa County Elections.[1]

NEWS.headline: U.S. Supreme Court ruling on late-arriving mail ballots doesn’t change Arizona law, Secretary Fontes says
NEWS.relevanceSummary: In this segment, Arizona Secretary of State Adrian Fontes explains that a new Supreme Court decision allowing states to count mail ballots that arrive after Election Day does not apply in Arizona, so AZ-06 voters must still ensure their mailed ballots are received by close of polls on Election Day under existing state law.[4]

NEWS.headline: Maricopa County tests election equipment; what voters should know
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Reports on Maricopa County’s public testing of tabulation scanners for vote centers and early ballots as the 2026 primary "officially kicked off," informing AZ-06 voters about the status and oversight of the voting machines used in their county.[2]

NEWS.headline: Early voting for the Arizona primary election starts next week
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Video segment outlines key dates and changes for the 2026 Arizona primary election, including the start of early voting and updated procedures, giving AZ-06 voters statewide deadlines and rules they must follow.[5]

NEWS.headline: How to vote in Arizona's 2026 primary election
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Explains 2026 Arizona primary voting procedures, including voter registration deadlines, party affiliation rules, acceptable voter ID, early voting, mail-in ballots, and how nonpartisan local races appear on the ballot, all of which apply statewide to voters in AZ-06.[8]

LAST_REFRESH.summary: Automated refresh completed 2026-07-08 for AZ-06. Sources checked: 21. LLM verifications: 10. No changes detected; all sources verified unchanged.

WEEKLY_REPORT.summary: Bi-weekly period June 3-17, 2026: First refresh for AZ-06 since the May 26 initial build. AZ SOS deadlines re-verified — unchanged. Ciscomani (R) vs. Mendoza (D) primary stretch confirmed; race remains a toss-up. New watch item: HCR 2001 voter-ID measure formally placed on the November 2026 ballot (June 15). HB 2492 proof-of-citizenship SCOTUS conflict still unresolved. No procedure changes; no confidence flips.
